Twitter's policies allowed broad API access until changes in 2023 restricted it, but prior lax enforcement enabled extensive data harvesting not tied to prominent UI features.
process_and_outcome: Twitter terminated thousands of developer accounts in 2023 and updated API terms to paid tiers with stricter data use rules. Contributed to FTC scrutiny amid broader platform changes.

example_name: Spotify Data Use for Discover Weekly
example_background: Spotify uses listening history and metadata to power user-facing features like Discover Weekly and Release Radar, which are prominently displayed on the app's home screen. Data collection is transparent via in-app privacy settings, and usage is confined to enhancing music discovery and personalization without sharing raw data externally for unrelated purposes. Users can control data retention and opt out of personalized recommendations directly in the UI.
process_and_outcome: No enforcement actions; Spotify's practices cited as a GDPR compliance model in 2021 Norwegian DPA audit, with minor recommendations implemented.

example_name: LinkedIn Data Scraping and HiQ Labs Case
example_background: HiQ Labs, a data analytics firm, accessed public LinkedIn profiles en masse using automated bots, collecting data on skills, employment history, and connections available through LinkedIn's public API scopes and web scraping. This raw data was aggregated to create workforce analytics products sold to clients. LinkedIn argued that even public data was subject to their User Agreement and API terms, which limited automated access and commercial use beyond what's permitted. HiQ used the data for predictions on employee churn without user consent for such derivations.
process_and_outcome: LinkedIn sued HiQ for breach of contract and CFAA violations. Initial injunctions favored LinkedIn, but the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals ruled in 2019 and 2022 that scraping public data does not violate the CFAA. The case settled in 2022 with HiQ agreeing to cease scraping LinkedIn data.
involved_parties: US Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit

example_background: Following the Schrems II ruling invalidating EU-US Privacy Shield, Microsoft adjusted its data practices for EU customers using services like Azure and Office 365. Data obtained via product scopes, such as user activity logs and telemetry, is stored in EU data centers with options for geo-fencing. Microsoft provides transparency reports on data requests and limits use of customer data to service improvement only, anonymizing aggregates for analytics. Customers can control data retention and export.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft achieved EU Cloud Code of Conduct certification and faced no major GDPR fines related to scope misuse. The company published detailed compliance reports and invested in sovereignty controls, serving as an industry benchmark.

example_name: Clearview AI Facial Recognition Data Harvesting
example_background: Clearview AI scraped billions of facial images from public websites, social media, and Google image searches without consent, building a database accessed via API scopes by law enforcement. The raw biometric data was aggregated into a searchable database, with derived matches sold or provided under subscription. This exceeded platform terms of service for Google Images and social sites, which limit automated bulk collection and commercial reuse of public data.
process_and_outcome: Canada fined Clearview AI CAD 20 million in 2022 under PIPEDA. Multiple US states investigated, and EU countries banned or fined the company. Clearview settled class actions and restricted sales amid ongoing lawsuits.
involved_parties: Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada

example_background: Salesforce implemented data masking and tokenization for customer data accessed via Salesforce APIs in production environments. Sensitive fields like emails and phone numbers obtained through CRM scopes are anonymized before use in training or testing datasets. Aggregated analytics are derived only from masked data, ensuring raw data from scopes remains limited to operational uses as per customer agreements.
process_and_outcome: Salesforce earned SOC 2 Type II compliance and ISO 27001 certifications. Customers report zero data misuse incidents, with tools like Shield Platform Encryption facilitating adherence.

example_name: TikTok GDPR Fine for Children's Data
example_background: TikTok, owned by ByteDance, collected biometric and location data from children's accounts without proper parental consent. The app's 'People Discovery' feature allowed data sharing with other users, and data was transferred to parent companies in China. From 2018 to 2020, TikTok processed data of users under 16 without age verification or consent mechanisms prominent in the user interface. Irish regulators found that default settings enabled data transfers without explicit opt-in, affecting millions of EU users.
process_and_outcome: In September 2023, the Irish Data Protection Commission fined TikTok €345 million for violations related to children's data processing and transfers. TikTok appealed but implemented new age gates and consent flows.

example_name: Clearview AI Scraping and Sales Violations
example_background: Clearview AI developed facial recognition technology by scraping billions of images from public websites, including social media platforms like Facebook and LinkedIn, without user consent. The company sold access to its database to law enforcement and private entities, transferring vast datasets. This bypassed platform policies on data extraction and lacked any user-facing consent mechanisms. European and Canadian regulators investigated after complaints from privacy advocates.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the Dutch DPA fined Clearview €30.5 million, and Canada's Privacy Commissioner ordered a halt to operations. Clearview faced bans in several EU countries and ongoing US litigation. The company continued operations in the US.
involved_parties: Canadian Office of the Privacy Commissioner

example_background: In 2021, Apple introduced App Tracking Transparency (ATT) in iOS 14.5, requiring apps to obtain explicit user consent via a prominent system prompt before tracking users across apps and websites for advertising. This framework ensures data transfers for tracking occur only with user approval displayed in a standardized, visible UI element. Apps like Facebook and Snapchat adapted by implementing the prompt, reducing cross-app tracking rates significantly as users opted out en masse.
process_and_outcome: ATT rollout led to a 30-50% drop in ad revenue for some platforms, but Apple avoided regulatory scrutiny and was praised for privacy enhancements. No fines issued; instead, it set an industry standard.

example_name: Meta GDPR Fine for Behavioral Advertising Transfers
example_background: Facebook (Meta) transferred EU user data to the US for behavioral advertising without adequate consent or safeguards post-Schrems II ruling. From 2018 to 2020, data including likes, shares, and inferred interests were sent to US servers without prominent UI consent or legal transfer mechanisms, affecting hundreds of millions of users. The Irish DPC found violations in data processing for ads.
process_and_outcome: In November 2022, the Irish DPC fined Meta €265 million. Meta appealed and updated its data transfer mechanisms with new Standard Contractual Clauses.

example_background: In 2022, Twitter shared user data via its API with third-party apps without sufficient consent controls. An internal tool called 'Cortex' authorized data transfers for AI training without prominent user notifications. Following the Musk acquisition, API access was restricted, but prior practices allowed broad data exports to partners like researcher accounts scraping millions of tweets.
process_and_outcome: EU regulators investigated under GDPR; Twitter agreed to audits. No fine issued yet, but enhanced API terms now require explicit consent for data use.

example_background: Signal, a privacy-focused messaging app, collects minimal user data and does not transfer data to third parties without explicit, prominent user consent in the UI. Features like contact uploads require opt-in prompts, and no data is shared for advertising or analytics. Even server logs are anonymized, and end-to-end encryption ensures no access to content. This approach has been used since 2014 and praised in privacy audits.
process_and_outcome: Signal has faced no major privacy fines and received endorsements from regulators and advocates. It served as a model during WhatsApp privacy policy controversies in 2021.

example_name: LinkedIn GDPR Fine for Data Transfers
example_background: LinkedIn transferred EU user data to the US for sales and marketing without valid transfer mechanisms after the Schrems II invalidation of Privacy Shield. Data included professional profiles, connections, and activity logs sent without granular, prominent consent in the user interface. The practice affected professional networking data from 2020-2021.
process_and_outcome: In October 2022, the Irish DPC fined LinkedIn €310 million for unlawful transfers. LinkedIn implemented new safeguards.

example_name: Twitter API Abuse by Developer Accounts
example_background: In 2022, Twitter suspended numerous developer accounts that were using the Twitter API to scrape public tweets and user data beyond the scopes permitted for their approved application types. Developers had registered apps claiming purposes like academic research or analytics but were found accessing full tweet timelines, user followers, and direct messages without justification. Twitter's developer policy specifies that apps must declare an appropriate use case matching permitted scopes, such as read-only access for display purposes or limited write access for posting. Violations included apps requesting 'read+write+directmessages' scopes for simple tweet display tools, leading to bulk data exports sold on data marketplaces.
process_and_outcome: Twitter terminated thousands of developer apps, suspended API keys, and banned offending accounts. The company updated its developer agreement in 2023 to introduce paid API tiers and stricter scope reviews. No monetary fines were publicly disclosed, but it contributed to Twitter's broader API policy overhaul under new ownership.

example_name: Google Play Pokemon Go Niantic Scope Overreach
example_background: Niantic's Pokemon Go app, launched in 2016, initially requested extensive Android permissions including full access to contacts, precise location, and storage. While not directly Google API scopes, the app integrated with Google Sign-In and Play Services, requesting scopes like profile and email that were used to link player accounts and enable social features. Critics noted that contact access was used for friend-finding features exceeding basic gameplay needs. Google Play policies require apps to justify permission requests matching their core functionality. Post-launch, privacy concerns led to backlash when users discovered data collection practices.
process_and_outcome: Niantic removed unnecessary permissions in updates following user complaints and media scrutiny. Google Play conducted reviews but issued no formal penalties. The incident prompted Google to tighten permission declarations in Play Console in 2017.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Google Play Developer Policy on Permissions

example_name: Microsoft Entra ID Conditional Access Success
example_background: Microsoft implemented strict scope controls in its Entra ID (formerly Azure AD) platform, where developers registering apps for Microsoft Graph API must select exact scopes like User.Read or Mail.Read matching their app's registered purpose, such as a CRM integration requesting only calendar access. Admin consent workflows review requests for scopes beyond delegated permissions. This approach ensures applications only access data appropriate to their type, preventing broad data harvesting. Microsoft publishes detailed documentation on permitted scopes per app type on its developer portal.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft's model has been audited and praised in compliance reports, with no major scope violation incidents reported. It serves as an industry benchmark, helping organizations achieve SOC 2 and ISO 27001 certifications.

example_background: Okta's developer platform requires apps to declare a use case (e.g., SSO integration, user management) and grants API scopes accordingly, such as okta.users.read-only for reporting apps. During app review, Okta verifies that requested scopes align with the described application type. Unauthorized scope usage triggers automatic token revocation. This granular control has been in place since Okta's early days, supporting thousands of integrations without notable overreach incidents.
process_and_outcome: Okta maintains a clean compliance record, with regular third-party audits confirming adherence. The policy contributed to its leadership in Gartner Magic Quadrant for Access Management.

example_name: Uber Google Maps API Unauthorized Use
example_background: Early versions of Uber's app used Google Maps JavaScript API without a proper premium license, embedding maps and requesting location data scopes beyond the free tier's permitted desktop-only use. Uber's mobile app accessed geolocation data for ride-matching, but violated terms by using the JS API key in a mobile webview. Google's policy limits JS API to web applications, requiring mobile SDKs for apps. This was discovered in 2015 during Google's billing audits.
process_and_outcome: Google issued a cease-and-desist, forcing Uber to migrate to Google Maps SDK for Android/iOS. Uber paid backdated licensing fees estimated in millions. No public fine, but it led to stricter API key enforcement across Google Cloud.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Google Maps Platform Terms of Service

example_name: Dropbox Business API Scope Compliance Program
example_background: Dropbox enforces a tiered scope system for its Business API, where apps must be approved for scopes like files.content.read based on their verified use case, such as backup tools requesting only metadata access. Developers submit app descriptions matching product types outlined in Dropbox's developer guidelines. Automated monitoring revokes tokens for scope misuse, ensuring access remains appropriate to the application's purpose.
process_and_outcome: Dropbox's program has prevented data breaches, earning FedRAMP authorization. It exemplifies secure API design, with case studies shared at developer conferences.

example_name: SEC Charges Against SolarWinds Orion Hackers' API Abuse
example_background: The 2020 SolarWinds supply chain attack involved nation-state actors compromising the Orion platform's update mechanism. While primarily a code injection, post-breach analysis revealed attackers used Orion's REST API with over-privileged service account tokens requesting full network discovery scopes unsuitable for monitoring tools. SolarWinds' API policy allowed broad scopes for enterprise management apps, which attackers exploited after initial access.
process_and_outcome: The SEC charged SolarWinds and its CISO in 2023 with disclosure failures, settling for $30 million. CISO paid $170,000 penalty. SolarWinds overhauled API access with least-privilege scopes.

example_background: In 2014, Facebook allowed an app developed by Aleksandr Kogan named 'thisisyourdigitallife' to collect data from approximately 270,000 users who consented to a personality quiz. The app also accessed data from millions of their Facebook friends without their knowledge or consent. Kogan shared this dataset with Cambridge Analytica, a political consulting firm, which used it for targeted political advertising during the 2016 US presidential election and Brexit referendum. The data included detailed profiles on up to 87 million users. Facebook's review processes failed to detect the unauthorized transfer, and the platform's data sharing policies permitted broad friend data access at the time. The incident came to light in 2018 through investigative journalism by The Guardian and The New York Times, revealing how personal data was harvested and applied in political influence campaigns without user notification or approval.
process_and_outcome: The US Federal Trade Commission (FTC) investigated and settled with Facebook in 2019 for $5 billion, the largest privacy penalty ever imposed, requiring structural changes to privacy practices and oversight. The UK Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) fined Facebook £500,000. Multiple class-action lawsuits were settled, and Facebook undertook a major overhaul of its data access APIs, third-party app reviews, and consent mechanisms. CEO Mark Zuckerberg testified before Congress.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Facebook Platform Policy on data usage and sharing

example_name: Equifax Data Breach and Sharing Incident
example_background: Equifax, one of the three major US credit bureaus, experienced a data breach in 2017 exposing sensitive personal information of 147 million people, including names, Social Security numbers, birth dates, addresses, and in some cases driver's license and credit card numbers. Attackers exploited a vulnerability in Apache Struts software that Equifax failed to patch despite known advisories. Post-breach, Equifax continued certain data sharing practices with affiliates and partners under its consumer notices, but the incident highlighted issues with data retention and third-party disclosures without explicit user opt-in for non-essential purposes. The company had shared consumer data with over 600 partners prior to the breach, raising questions about consent granularity.
process_and_outcome: The US Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) fined Equifax $575 million, alongside settlements with FTC ($100 million), 50 state AGs ($425 million consumer fund), and UK ICO (£500,000). Equifax implemented enhanced security, free credit monitoring, and data minimization policies. Class-action lawsuits resulted in $1.38 billion in settlements.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Equifax Privacy Policy on data sharing

example_name: Microsoft Azure Enterprise Data Sharing Compliance
example_background: Microsoft's Azure cloud platform includes enterprise data processing agreements that outline customer data handling. For security monitoring and abuse prevention, Microsoft scans customer content in Azure for malware and compliance with abuse policies. Customer data is not shared with third parties except for legal compliance or with explicit customer consent in enterprise contracts. During mergers like the Activision Blizzard acquisition, Microsoft obtained regulatory approvals and user notifications where applicable, ensuring data portability consents were handled per policy. This setup has supported thousands of enterprise customers without major incidents.
process_and_outcome: No enforcement actions; Microsoft maintained compliance through regular SOC 2 audits and ISO certifications. In 2023, Azure achieved FedRAMP High authorization, demonstrating adherence in government contracts.
involved_parties: US Federal Risk and Authorization Management Program (FedRAMP)

example_name: TikTok Data Sharing with ByteDance Parent
example_background: TikTok, owned by Chinese company ByteDance, faced allegations of sharing US user data with ByteDance employees in China without explicit user consent. Internal audits revealed that from 2018-2020, data access logs showed ByteDance engineers accessing US user data for algorithmic improvements and moderation. TikTok's privacy policy allowed sharing with affiliates for operational purposes, but lacked granular consent for cross-border transfers. This led to national security concerns, especially regarding potential government access under Chinese law.
process_and_outcome: US CFIUS ordered divestiture of TikTok's US operations in 2020 (later paused). FTC investigated privacy claims, resulting in a 2022 settlement where TikTok paid $92 million to Illinois users over biometric data. TikTok implemented Project Texas, isolating US data in Oracle clouds with no China access.

example_name: Apple App Store Data Handling During Acquisition
example_background: Apple's App Store policies require developers to obtain explicit user consent before sharing personal data with third parties, except for security, legal compliance, or with prior consent for mergers. In Apple's 2014 acquisition of Beats Electronics, user data from Beats Music was migrated to Apple Music only after obtaining explicit opt-in consents from subscribers. Apple provided clear notifications and 30-day opt-out periods, ensuring data was not transferred without approval. This process aligned with privacy commitments during the asset sale.
process_and_outcome: The acquisition closed smoothly without regulatory challenges on privacy grounds. Apple Music launched successfully, and subsequent privacy audits confirmed compliance.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Apple App Store Review Guidelines (5.1.1 Data Collection)
EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Article 6 (consent)

example_name: LinkedIn Data Scraping and Sharing Violations
example_background: hiQ Labs, a data analytics firm, scraped public LinkedIn profiles without user consent to build datasets sold to recruiters. LinkedIn's User Agreement prohibited scraping, and data sharing occurred without explicit permissions. From 2017-2019, hiQ accessed profiles of 300 million+ users, compiling skill and employment data shared with enterprise clients. The case tested boundaries of public data usage versus platform policies.
process_and_outcome: US Ninth Circuit ruled in favor of hiQ initially (2019), but Supreme Court vacated (2021). LinkedIn banned hiQ, enhanced anti-scraping measures, and settled related suits. hiQ ceased operations in 2022.
involved_parties: US Court of Appeals Ninth Circuit

example_background: Salesforce provides cloud CRM services under Data Processing Addenda (DPAs) that permit customer data processing for service delivery, security monitoring (e.g., abuse detection), and legal compliance. Data sharing with sub-processors requires notification and customer approval. During Salesforce's acquisitions like Tableau (2019), explicit consents were obtained for data integration, with opt-outs offered. No unauthorized sharing occurred, and regular compliance reports demonstrate adherence.
process_and_outcome: Salesforce maintains GDPR adequacy decisions and SOC compliance without major fines. Annual transparency reports detail legal data requests.

example_name: Twitter (X) Data Sharing with Advertisers Breach
example_background: In 2022, Twitter was found sharing user data with advertisers through its Tailored Audiences and Partner Audiences programs without always obtaining explicit prior consent for hashed email/phone matching. Internal audits post-Elon Musk acquisition revealed non-compliance with GDPR for EU users, where data was shared for ad targeting without granular opt-ins. This affected millions of users whose contact info was uploaded by partners for retargeting.
process_and_outcome: Ireland's Data Protection Commission (DPC) fined Twitter €450,000 in 2023 under GDPR for unlawful processing. Twitter suspended the programs temporarily and updated consent flows.

example_name: Meta Platforms Cambridge Analytica Data Incident
example_background: In 2014, an application called This Is Your Digital Life, developed by Aleksandr Kogan, was installed by approximately 300,000 Facebook users. The application collected data from those users as well as from up to 87 million users' friends without their knowledge or consent. Kogan shared this dataset with Cambridge Analytica, a political consulting firm, which used it for targeted political advertising during the 2016 US presidential election and the Brexit referendum. Facebook allowed third-party apps to access friend data until 2014-2015 policy changes. The data was not aggregated or used solely for internal operations, nor justified by legal compliance or security needs. Facebook learned of the unauthorized sharing in 2015 but did not notify affected users until 2018 following media reports by The Guardian and The New York Times.
process_and_outcome: The US Federal Trade Commission (FTC) investigated and settled with Facebook for $5 billion in penalties and injunctive relief requiring enhanced privacy practices. The UK Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) fined Facebook £500,000. Multiple class-action lawsuits were settled, and Facebook undertook a comprehensive privacy program overhaul including data access audits.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Facebook Platform Policy on data use

example_background: In 2018, France's data protection authority CNIL investigated Google's processing of personal data for personalized advertising services in Android and web environments. Google was found to have insufficient legal basis for processing, lack of transparency in combining data across services (e.g., YouTube, Google Search, Maps), and failure to obtain valid consent. The processing involved detailed behavioral profiles for over 49 million French users without aggregation for internal use or legal mandates. Subsequent investigations in other EU countries followed similar patterns regarding cookie consent and ad targeting.
process_and_outcome: CNIL imposed a €50 million fine, the first under GDPR. Additional fines came from regulators in the UK (£7.5 million for Nexus Media), Belgium (€10,000), and others totaling over €100 million. Google appealed but lost, leading to policy adjustments in consent banners.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU GDPR (Articles 6, 7 on lawful basis and consent)

example_background: TikTok collected personal data from children under 13 without verifiable parental consent through its For You page algorithm, which served content to underage users without age gating. Data included location, device info, and behavioral signals used for personalized feeds. The company claimed internal aggregation for operations but regulators found it exceeded permissible uses under children's privacy laws.
process_and_outcome: US FTC settled for $5.7 million, the largest ever for child privacy violations. TikTok implemented age verification, parental controls, and data deletion processes. Additional Irish DPC investigation ongoing into EU practices.

example_background: Microsoft Azure and Microsoft 365 services process customer data with strict controls, retaining data only for service provision, legal compliance (e.g., subpoenas), or aggregated analytics for internal improvements like threat detection. Data is encrypted at rest and in transit, with customer control over retention and no use for unrelated advertising.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft achieved multiple certifications and faced no major fines for data misuse. Annual transparency reports detail government requests (e.g., 18,000+ in H1 2023), showing adherence. Customer audits confirm compliance.

example_name: Google Nest Cam Data Sharing Consent Issue
example_background: Google's Nest Secure home security system inadvertently allowed certain Nest account holders to access video footage from other users' cameras due to a software glitch discovered in 2019. This occurred because the system did not properly segment user data, leading to instances where individuals could view live streams and recordings from unrelated households without any prior authorization or notification. The issue affected a limited number of users but highlighted challenges in isolating personal surveillance data in connected devices. Google became aware through user reports and internal monitoring.
process_and_outcome: Google disabled the Nest Secure feature in 2020 and discontinued the product line. No formal regulatory fines were imposed, but the incident prompted enhanced data isolation protocols and user notifications about the discontinuation.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Google API Services User Data Policy

example_background: Apple introduced HealthKit in iOS 8 in 2014, allowing users to store and share health and fitness data from various apps and devices. Access to this data requires explicit user permission for each app, with granular controls specifying which data types (e.g., heart rate, steps, medical records) can be read or written. Apps must request permission through system prompts, and users can revoke access at any time via settings. This framework ensures that no health data is shared without the user's affirmative opt-in for specific data points and recipients.
process_and_outcome: Apple's approach has been recognized in privacy audits and reports as setting industry standards, avoiding major incidents or fines related to health data mishandling. It continues to evolve with features like Health Sharing in iOS 16.

example_background: Facebook launched Messenger Kids in 2017 as a child-safe messaging app, but New Mexico's Attorney General investigated after finding that the app collected persistent device identifiers and geolocation data from children under 13 without obtaining verifiable parental consent. The data was sent to Facebook servers, potentially enabling persistent tracking across sessions. Parents were not adequately informed about the extent of data collection, and the app's terms did not clearly disclose these practices for children's data.
process_and_outcome: In 2019, Facebook settled with New Mexico for $5.5 million and agreed to overhaul child privacy practices, including better parental notifications and data minimization.

example_background: Microsoft Azure implements confidential computing features using hardware-based Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs) like Intel SGX and AMD SEV. Customer data processed in these environments remains encrypted in use, inaccessible even to Microsoft personnel. Before any data processing, users explicitly configure and approve access policies through the Azure portal, specifying which services or users can interact with the data under strict attestation protocols.
process_and_outcome: This has enabled compliance in regulated industries, with no major breaches attributed to improper data access. Recognized in NIST reports on confidential computing.

example_background: Amazon's Ring doorbell camera service employed contractors who accessed and annotated customer video footage without explicit customer consent for training AI models. Internal documents revealed that some employees discussed specific videos inappropriately, and videos from thousands of users were viewed. Customers were not informed that their private home videos could be accessed by humans for quality assurance and improvement purposes beyond what was disclosed in vague terms of service.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the FTC settled with Ring for $5.8 million, requiring deletion of unviewed videos, implementation of strict access controls, and customer notifications for human review.

example_background: Signal Messenger requires users to explicitly approve contacts and devices that can access message metadata or initiate calls. Message content is end-to-end encrypted, and no human at Signal can access user data without user-approved linked devices. Users receive clear prompts for each new device linkage, and data is not readable by the service provider under any circumstances.
process_and_outcome: Signal has faced no major privacy enforcement actions and is endorsed by privacy advocates for its model.

example_background: TikTok's parent ByteDance collected biometric data, including facial recognition scans from video filters, from millions of US users without obtaining clear, affirmative consent. The app's terms did not specify that such data would be extracted and stored, and users under 16 were allowed access without parental verification in some cases.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, TikTok settled class-action lawsuits for $92 million over Illinois Biometric Information Privacy Act (BIPA) claims.

example_background: In 2022, the French data protection authority investigated Google's processing of personal data for advertising purposes under its Privacy Sandbox initiative and other targeted ad practices. The probe focused on the handling of user consent mechanisms and the transfer of user location and behavioral data to third-party ad networks without explicit, granular consent from users across the European Economic Area. Google's systems collected identifiers such as IP addresses, device information, and browsing history, which were shared with external platforms for real-time bidding in ad auctions, affecting millions of users who were defaulted into data processing without clear opt-in options.
process_and_outcome: The CNIL issued a €150 million fine in 2020 for cookie consent issues and escalated to a €1.375 billion penalty in 2022 after finding continued data transfers to US-based ad partners without adequate safeguards. Google challenged the decision in court but ultimately paid the fine and committed to enhancing consent tools.

example_background: Meta's platforms, including Facebook and Instagram, transferred European user data including profile details, interests, and interaction histories to the United States for ad targeting and analytics. The transfers involved standard contractual clauses that were deemed insufficient under Schrems II ruling, as US surveillance laws allowed access to data by intelligence agencies without equivalent protections. This affected over 500 million users' data flows to Meta's US servers for processing by ad partners and internal systems.
process_and_outcome: The Irish Data Protection Commission led the investigation and imposed a record €1.2 billion fine in 2023, ordering Meta to suspend future transfers and bring existing data back to the EU within five months. Meta appealed but suspended certain transfers pending resolution.

example_background: TikTok's app collected biometric data, location information, and behavioral signals from users under 16 without parental consent, sharing this data with third-party analytics and advertising firms. The platform's age-gating was bypassed, leading to data from approximately 580,000 children being processed and transferred to entities outside China for algorithmic content and ad personalization.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC fined TikTok €345 million in 2023 following a multi-year probe, requiring changes to default privacy settings and data deletion for affected minors. TikTok agreed to comply and appealed aspects of the decision.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 8 special conditions for children's consent

example_background: Clearview AI scraped billions of facial images from public websites and social media platforms, compiling a database sold to law enforcement and private clients including ad tech firms for identity verification and targeting. EU regulators found the company transferred scraped biometric data across borders without consent, violating data minimization and purpose limitation as images were repurposed for commercial resale.
process_and_outcome: Multiple EU DPAs banned processing and fined Clearview €30.5 million collectively in 2022-2023; the company was ordered to cease operations in the EU and delete unlawfully held data.

example_background: Amazon's advertising services used data from its marketplace, including purchase histories, search queries, and viewing patterns, to fuel targeted ads shared with third-party sellers and external networks. The Luxembourg regulator examined how non-household data was combined with personal data for personalization without valid legal basis or consent, impacting tens of millions of EU users.
process_and_outcome: The CNPD fined Amazon €746 million in 2021; Amazon appealed to the CJEU, arguing legitimate interest justified processing, with the case ongoing as of 2024.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Articles 6 and 9 lawful basis for processing

example_background: Microsoft provides users with a privacy dashboard in its services like Windows, Office 365, and Azure, allowing granular control over data collection for ads and analytics. Data sharing with partners is restricted to essential functions with opt-out options, and transfers to third parties like ad brokers are prohibited without consent. This applies to billions of data points from enterprise and consumer users.
process_and_outcome: No major fines; recognized in privacy rankings by Forbes and IAPP for transparency, with ongoing audits confirming adherence.

example_background: LinkedIn processed professional profile data, skills, and connection graphs for ad targeting, transferring this information to US servers and sharing with third-party ad platforms without adequate consent banners or transparency. The practice involved behavioral advertising based on inferred interests from user activity, affecting EU professionals.
process_and_outcome: Luxembourg CNPD fined LinkedIn €310 million in 2022 for lack of legal basis; LinkedIn updated its consent mechanisms in response.

example_name: Google Revokes YouTube API Access from TikTok
example_background: In 2020, Google suspended TikTok's access to YouTube's Data API after TikTok failed to complete required security assessments and verification processes for accessing sensitive user data scopes. TikTok's application was processing millions of user grants without demonstrating adherence to Google's security practices for restricted scopes, which include annual third-party security assessments and Letters of Assessment. This action followed Google's detection during routine compliance reviews that TikTok had not renewed its verification status despite high-volume data access.
process_and_outcome: Google immediately suspended API access, requiring TikTok to undergo full re-verification. TikTok completed the process after several months, regaining access only after passing the security assessment. No fines were imposed, but access was disrupted for months.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Google API Services User Data Policy
YouTube Data API Terms of Service

example_name: Twitter Suspended from Google Maps APIs
example_background: In 2018, Twitter (now X) lost access to Google Maps APIs, including Places API, due to failure to maintain annual security assessments and comply with data handling requirements for sensitive location data scopes. Twitter was handling data from numerous user grants without current third-party assessments, violating Google's policies for applications accessing restricted scopes.
process_and_outcome: Google terminated API access abruptly. Twitter negotiated reinstatement after conducting required security audits and obtaining a Letter of Assessment, resuming service after remediation.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Google Maps Platform Terms of Service
API Security and Data Handling Policy

example_background: In 2018, it was revealed that Cambridge Analytica accessed data from millions of Facebook users through a third-party app called 'thisisyourdigitallife' without proper security safeguards or user consent verification. The app collected sensitive profile data, friends' data, and behavioral information, which was handled insecurely and shared with political consulting firms. Facebook's review found the app had not undergone required security assessments for handling large-scale sensitive data access.
process_and_outcome: Facebook banned Cambridge Analytica from its platform. UK and US regulators investigated, leading to FTC settlement with Facebook for $5 billion and Cambridge Analytica's bankruptcy. Extensive remediation of app verification processes followed.

example_background: Microsoft implemented a rigorous verification program for Microsoft Graph API apps accessing sensitive scopes like Mail.Read and User.Read.All, requiring third-party security assessments, annual re-verification, and detailed data handling documentation for apps with high user grants, mirroring Google's OAuth practices.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft successfully enforced compliance, revoking access from non-compliant apps and maintaining platform security. Approved apps receive verified badges, demonstrating ongoing adherence.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Microsoft Graph Permissions and Consent Framework

example_background: Salesforce requires Connected Apps accessing sensitive data scopes to undergo annual security reviews, penetration testing by approved third parties, and provide Letters of Authorization, similar to Google's model for high-risk integrations handling customer data.
process_and_outcome: Program has prevented breaches by ensuring only verified apps access data, with regular audits maintaining compliance across thousands of integrations.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Salesforce Connected App Security Review Policy

example_background: Okta mandates security assessments and continuous monitoring for applications accessing sensitive identity data scopes through its API, requiring annual third-party audits and compliance documentation for integrations serving large user bases.
process_and_outcome: Okta's program identified and remediated risks proactively, maintaining zero major API-related breaches and serving as industry benchmark.

example_background: In 2011, Google reached a settlement related to its launch of Google Buzz in 2010, a social networking feature integrated into Gmail. The service automatically enrolled Gmail users by displaying their most frequently emailed contacts publicly in the Buzz stream without obtaining explicit opt-in consent. This exposed email addresses and contact lists to other users and third parties. The feature also enabled tracking of user interactions for advertising purposes. Users could opt out, but the default enrollment raised concerns about unauthorized sharing of personal contact information, which was used in conjunction with ad personalization mechanisms. The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) investigated after receiving complaints from privacy advocates and users who found their private contacts suddenly public and subject to data collection for targeted ads.
process_and_outcome: The FTC settled with Google for $22.5 million, the largest civil penalty it had ever obtained from a company at that time. Google was required to establish and maintain a comprehensive privacy program, undergo biennial third-party audits for 20 years, and clearly disclose changes to privacy practices. No admission of liability was made, but the settlement set a precedent for privacy enforcement.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: FTC Act Section 5 (unfair and deceptive practices)

example_background: In 2018, it was revealed that Cambridge Analytica, a political consulting firm, accessed data from up to 87 million Facebook users without their consent through a third-party app called 'thisisyourdigitallife' developed by researcher Aleksandr Kogan. The app collected detailed profile information from users who installed it and extended data collection to their Facebook friends' networks. This data was then used to build psychological profiles for targeted political advertising during the 2016 U.S. presidential election and Brexit campaigns. Facebook's platform allowed such data harvesting due to lax developer policies, and the company failed to notify affected users or prevent the data's transfer and use for ad targeting. The incident highlighted issues with user data sharing for advertising purposes beyond user knowledge.
process_and_outcome: The FTC fined Facebook $5 billion in 2019, the largest privacy-related fine ever imposed. Additional penalties included a $725 million class-action settlement in 2022 and a £90 million UK fine. Facebook implemented new data access restrictions for apps and enhanced user controls, with CEO Mark Zuckerberg testifying before Congress.

example_background: TikTok, owned by ByteDance, faced scrutiny for collecting personal data from children under 13 via its Musical.ly app before its merger into TikTok in 2018. The app allowed kids to create and share short videos, during which it gathered names, locations, and device information without verifiable parental consent. This data was used to serve personalized video recommendations and interest-based ads. Despite age gates, many children accessed the app, and the platform's algorithms facilitated data collection for advertising revenue. The FTC alleged the company knew of underage usage but continued data practices that targeted minors.
process_and_outcome: In 2019, TikTok agreed to pay $5.7 million to settle FTC charges, the largest civil penalty for children's privacy violations at the time. ByteDance was ordered to implement a robust children's privacy program, delete pre-2019 child data, and comply with COPPA requirements for age screening and parental notices.

example_name: In re: Facebook, Inc. Consumer Privacy User Profile Litigation
example_background: Facebook users filed a class-action lawsuit alleging the company tracked their internet activity across third-party websites using 'Like' buttons and tracking pixels, even when not logged in. This data, including browsing history on non-Facebook sites, was combined with profile information to create detailed dossiers for hyper-personalized advertising and retargeting. The tracking occurred without explicit consent, violating privacy representations in Facebook's policies. The case covered millions of users whose data was sold to advertisers for behavioral targeting from 2010 to 2018.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, Meta agreed to a $725 million settlement, one of the largest in a data privacy class action. Users could claim payments, and Meta enhanced tracking disclosures and opt-out tools. The Ninth Circuit had previously certified the class.

example_name: Google EU Antitrust Fine for AdTech Practices
example_background: The European Commission investigated Google's advertising technology stack, finding that Google gave illegal advantages to its own ad services like AdX and DoubleClick for Publishers. Between 2014 and 2018, Google used data from its search and Android ecosystems to unfairly compete in ad auctions, preventing competitors from using the same first-party data for bidding. This involved transferring publisher user data across Google's services for personalized ad targeting without equivalent access for rivals, consolidating market power in online advertising.
process_and_outcome: In 2024, the Commission proposed a fine of up to 1.49 billion euros ($1.6 billion), with ongoing proceedings. Google committed to changes in ad auction processes but faces potential penalties and remedies to open data access.

example_background: In 2021, Apple introduced App Tracking Transparency (ATT), requiring apps to obtain explicit user permission before tracking them across apps and websites for advertising. This addressed industry practices where apps accessed the Advertising Identifier (IDFA) to build user profiles from location, purchase, and behavioral data for retargeting ads. Apple's policy shift limited third-party ad networks' ability to use such data without consent, reducing cross-app tracking revenue for companies like Meta by billions.
process_and_outcome: ATT rollout led to 80-90% user opt-out rates in some apps, forcing advertisers to adopt privacy-preserving alternatives like aggregated reporting. No fines were imposed on Apple; it positioned as a privacy leader, with iOS 14.5+ enforcing the framework globally.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Apple App Store Review Guidelines 5.1.3 (Data Collection)

example_background: Brave Browser, launched in 2016, blocks third-party trackers and ads by default while offering an opt-in rewards system where users view privacy-preserving ads. These ads use local processing on the user's device without sharing behavioral data with servers or advertisers. Unlike traditional models, no user data is transferred or sold for retargeting; instead, users earn Basic Attention Tokens (BAT) for engagement, and advertisers bid anonymously based on aggregate cohorts.
process_and_outcome: Brave grew to over 50 million monthly users by 2023 without regulatory penalties. It demonstrated a viable alternative to data-driven ads, influencing industry shifts toward contextual advertising and earning praise from privacy regulators.

example_name: ProPublica Report on Facebook Data for Political Ads
example_background: Investigative reporting in 2018 revealed Facebook allowed political advertisers to target users using highly sensitive data categories like 'behavior: away from married' or 'in friendship: Republican primary.' These off-platform data lists were uploaded and matched against Facebook user profiles for micro-targeted ads, enabling precise voter manipulation without disclosure. The practice involved selling access to user data for interest-based political advertising, raising election integrity concerns.
process_and_outcome: Facebook suspended 'partner categories' in 2019 and banned political data targeting tools. It faced congressional scrutiny and contributed to broader platform changes, though no direct fines resulted from this specific report.

example_name: New York AG Suit Against Google Location Data Sales
example_background: Google was accused of misleading users about location data collection post-2018 privacy updates. Despite users turning off 'Location History,' Google still tracked locations via other services like web/activity data and sold this to advertisers for location-based retargeting ads. The data included visits to sensitive sites like clinics and religious centers, aggregated into profiles for personalized advertising without clear consent.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, Google settled with New York AG for $391.5 million and agreed to clearer disclosures and controls. Additional multistate settlements totaled nearly $400 million, with mandated audits.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: New York General Business Law § 349 (deceptive practices)

example_name: Equifax Data Breach and Credit Reporting
example_background: In 2017, Equifax, one of the three major consumer credit reporting agencies in the United States, experienced a massive data breach affecting approximately 147 million people. Hackers exploited a vulnerability in a web application to access sensitive personal information, including names, Social Security numbers, birth dates, addresses, and in some cases driver's license numbers and credit card details. The stolen data was used for identity theft and fraudulent credit applications. Equifax's role as a credit bureau involved collecting and using consumer data from various sources, including financial institutions and public records, to generate credit reports and scores used by lenders for creditworthiness determinations. The breach occurred because the company delayed patching a known Apache Struts vulnerability despite warnings from the Department of Homeland Security. This incident highlighted issues with handling user data intended for credit and lending decisions, as the compromised data directly impacted creditworthiness assessments across the financial sector.
process_and_outcome: The U.S. Federal Trade Commission (FTC), Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB), and multiple state attorneys general investigated. Equifax agreed to a settlement exceeding $700 million, including up to $425 million for consumer compensation, $100 million in FTC penalties, $175 million to states, and $125 million to the CFPB. The company also committed to enhancing data security practices and providing free credit monitoring to affected consumers for multiple years.

example_name: Upstart AI Lending Algorithm CFPB Action
example_background: Upstart, a fintech company using AI and machine learning for lending decisions, came under scrutiny for its use of alternative data sources beyond traditional credit scores. The company's models incorporated over 1,600 variables, including education, employment history, and other consumer data, to assess creditworthiness. In 2023, the CFPB examined whether these practices complied with fair lending laws, as the use of non-traditional data raised concerns about potential transfers or uses of user data from various sources for credit decisions without adequate transparency or consent mechanisms.
process_and_outcome: The CFPB issued a supervisory report highlighting risks in AI lending models. Upstart adjusted its practices, and no enforcement action was taken at the time, but it led to industry-wide guidance on algorithmic transparency.

example_background: In 2019, Apple launched the Apple Card in partnership with Goldman Sachs and Mastercard, using algorithmic models to determine credit limits and approvals. Comedian David Spade and his wife publicly noted that she received a credit limit 20 times lower than his despite similar finances, sparking claims of gender bias in the AI-driven creditworthiness assessments. The model used Apple user data, transaction history, and other signals to make lending decisions, raising questions about opaque use of personal data for credit purposes.
process_and_outcome: The New York Department of Financial Services (NYDFS) investigated Goldman Sachs. The bank revised its algorithms, improved transparency, and faced a $25 million fine in 2023 for unrelated debt collection issues, with gender bias probes ongoing.

example_name: CFPB Action Against Portfolio Recovery Associates
example_background: Portfolio Recovery Associates (PRA), a debt buyer, purchased consumer debts and used data analytics to pursue collections and infer creditworthiness for further lending. The CFPB found that PRA used unreliable data, including inflated or unsubstantiated debt amounts, sourced from original creditors, to make credit reporting decisions affecting consumers' credit scores and lending opportunities.
process_and_outcome: In 2017, the CFPB ordered PRA to pay over $24 million in redress and penalties and improve data validation processes before reporting to credit bureaus.

example_name: Zest AI Fair Lending Compliance Program
example_background: Zest AI provides AI-based underwriting software to lenders, enabling the use of alternative data for credit decisions while maintaining explainability and fairness. The company conducts ongoing audits and provides tools for lenders to validate models against disparate impact, ensuring data used for creditworthiness does not violate anti-discrimination laws. This approach demonstrates structured handling of user data sourced from APIs and other integrations for lending without unauthorized transfers.
process_and_outcome: Zest AI received positive evaluations from regulators and expanded partnerships with community banks, avoiding enforcement actions through proactive compliance testing.

example_name: Plaid Data Sharing Settlement with FTC
example_background: Plaid, a fintech company providing bank account aggregation via APIs (including connections to services like Venmo), was sued by the FTC for deceptive practices. Plaid scraped user data from financial apps without clear consent and sold access to this data to lenders and others for purposes including credit underwriting and cash advance approvals, mimicking login credentials to access sensitive financial information.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, Plaid settled for $58 million with the FTC and class actions, agreeing to limit data retention and enhance user consent mechanisms for API-based data sharing.

example_background: Finicity, owned by Mastercard, operates an open banking platform using APIs to connect consumer bank data to lenders for credit decisions and account verification. In 2022, the CFPB scrutinized its practices for collecting and sharing consumer financial data without adequate consumer notices or opt-out options, facilitating lending assessments.
process_and_outcome: The CFPB proposed rules partly in response, and Finicity enhanced disclosures. No direct fine, but it led to industry changes in data broker consents.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: CFPB Personal Financial Data Rights Rule (proposed)

example_name: Cambridge Analytica Google API Data Harvesting
example_background: Cambridge Analytica, a political consulting firm, developed an application called 'thisisyourdigitallife' that was distributed via Facebook. The app collected data from approximately 87 million Facebook users without their explicit consent by leveraging Facebook's API services. This data included profile information, likes, and friend networks. The firm then used this data for targeted political advertising during the 2016 US presidential election and the Brexit referendum. The method involved users granting permissions through the app, which then accessed data from their networks. Google services were implicated in parts of the data processing and analytics pipeline, where APIs were used to handle and analyze large datasets, potentially exceeding intended usage scopes outlined in service terms. The incident raised questions about compliance with platform policies on data access and usage.
process_and_outcome: Investigations by the UK Information Commissioner's Office, US Federal Trade Commission, and others led to Cambridge Analytica ceasing operations in 2018. Facebook faced a $5 billion settlement with the FTC. API access restrictions were tightened across platforms, with suspensions of related developer accounts.

example_name: YouTube API Suspension for Copyleaks Plagiarism Checker
example_background: Copyleaks, an AI-based plagiarism detection service, integrated the YouTube Data API to scan video transcripts for academic integrity checks. The service allowed educators to submit YouTube video links, after which it fetched transcripts via the API and analyzed content against academic papers. This usage reportedly exceeded the API's quota limits and involved systematic data extraction for commercial purposes without prior approval. Google identified the activity as scraping-like behavior, contravening terms that prohibit excessive or unauthorized data collection.
process_and_outcome: Google suspended Copyleaks' access to the YouTube Data API in 2023. The company appealed but was denied, forcing a pivot to alternative data sourcing methods. Public discussions highlighted the need for clearer quota guidelines.

example_name: Twitter API Access Revocation for Third-Party Apps
example_background: In 2023, following changes in API policies under new ownership, Twitter (now X) restricted free API access and imposed tiered pricing. Numerous third-party applications, such as TweetDeck alternatives and analytics tools, relied on the API for real-time data feeds, user interactions, and content aggregation. Many apps exceeded the new rate limits or failed to upgrade to paid tiers, leading to widespread suspensions. This affected developers who had previously operated within legacy terms but were now out of compliance with updated restrictions on data usage and redistribution.
process_and_outcome: Twitter enforced suspensions on non-compliant apps, with over 100 third-party services losing access. Developers faced abrupt service disruptions, prompting some to shut down operations or migrate platforms.

example_name: Google Revokes API Access to Musk's xAI Grok
example_background: xAI, founded by Elon Musk, developed the Grok AI chatbot which utilized Google Maps APIs for location-based functionalities within its responses. Reports indicated that the integration involved high-volume queries that triggered Google's monitoring systems. The usage pattern suggested potential overages in quota and terms violations related to caching and redistribution of API data in AI-generated outputs, breaching policies on data usage for machine learning training.
process_and_outcome: Google suspended xAI's access to Google Maps APIs in 2024. xAI publicly acknowledged the suspension and sought alternatives, highlighting tensions between AI developers and API providers.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Google Maps Platform Terms of Service

example_name: Anthropic Google Gemini API Compliance Model
example_background: Anthropic, an AI safety-focused company, integrated Google's Gemini API into its Claude models under strict adherence to terms. They implemented rate limiting, user consent mechanisms, and data minimization practices. Usage was confined to approved scopes, with regular audits to ensure no data was used for unauthorized training or redistribution. This approach served as a model for responsible API integration in generative AI applications.
process_and_outcome: Anthropic maintained uninterrupted access and received Google's partnership recognition. They published guidelines on compliant API usage, influencing industry standards.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Google API Services: User Data Policy

example_name: OpenAI Google Custom Search API Suspension
example_background: OpenAI's ChatGPT plugin ecosystem included tools that leveraged Google's Custom Search JSON API to fetch real-time web data. High query volumes from users led to quota exhaustion and detection of patterns resembling scraping, which violated terms prohibiting bulk data extraction for AI training or commercial aggregation without enterprise licensing.
process_and_outcome: Google suspended OpenAI's API keys in 2023, prompting OpenAI to disable affected plugins and negotiate enterprise terms. This incident spurred broader discussions on API usage in LLMs.

example_name: Perplexity AI Google Search API Enforcement
example_background: Perplexity AI, an AI-powered search engine, utilized Google Search APIs to power its query responses and citations. The service processed millions of daily queries, leading to excessive API calls that exceeded fair use policies. Data from the API was incorporated into proprietary models, raising issues with terms on data caching, redistribution, and usage for competitive products.
process_and_outcome: Google flagged and restricted Perplexity's API access in 2024 amid broader scrutiny of AI firms. Perplexity shifted to custom crawlers, but faced ongoing legal challenges from publishers.

example_name: Salesforce Einstein TrustLayer Google API Integration
example_background: Salesforce implemented its Einstein TrustLayer framework to ensure compliant usage of Google Workspace APIs in its CRM platform. This included automated compliance checks, consent management, and scoped access tokens limited to enterprise-approved data. The integration supported secure data syncing for customer analytics without violating user data policies.
process_and_outcome: Salesforce achieved Google Cloud Partner status with no suspensions. The framework was certified compliant, enabling scaled deployments across enterprise clients.

example_name: Clearview AI Google Image Search API Misuse
example_background: Clearview AI built a facial recognition database by scraping billions of images from public web sources, including heavy reliance on Google Image Search APIs to discover and index URLs. The systematic querying violated rate limits and terms against using APIs for surveillance or database building without permission. Data was compiled into a commercial product sold to law enforcement.
process_and_outcome: Google terminated Clearview's API access in 2020. Multiple lawsuits followed, with fines in Europe under GDPR totaling over €30 million. US privacy regulators investigated ongoing.
